nativeRange.habitat: Small, shaded blackwater streams and pools in lowland and hill forest; slow-moving or still water over leaf litter with minimal water movement; heavily shaded by dense canopy

decorPreferences: dense leaf litter (Indian almond leaves, oak leaves) to soften water and provide tannins
floating plants (water sprite, frogbit) for surface cover and shade
driftwood and twisted roots for cover and territory delineation
dim, diffuse lighting — avoid bright light
peat filtration or RO water to achieve soft, acidic blackwater conditions
minimal flow — gentle sponge filter preferred

diet.notes: Primarily live and frozen invertebrates: live blackworms, bloodworms, daphnia, brine shrimp, micro-worms, and small crickets or fruit flies. Dried and prepared foods are often refused initially; conditioning onto frozen bloodworms or frozen daphnia is possible but requires persistence. Wild fish feed on small invertebrates, insects, and zooplankton. Overfeeding must be avoided in soft-acid water as organic waste rapidly degrades water quality.

goodWith: conspecific pair (one male, one female — species-only tank strongly recommended)
small, peaceful, unobtrusive bottom dwellers in very large tanks (e.g., small Corydoras if water parameters align — rarely practical)
avoidWith: active or fin-nipping species (guppies, tetras, barbs)
any species requiring harder or more alkaline water

Wikimedia Commons category for Betta macrostoma. Several CC-licensed images of wild-type males in breeding coloration available.

summary: The Spotfin Betta (Brunei Beauty) is the largest wild Betta species, endemic to Brunei’s blackwater streams. A paternal mouthbrooder requiring extreme soft-acid conditions (pH 4.0–6.5, gH 0–3), it is among the most demanding and expensive Bettas in the hobby, suited only to dedicated specialists.
careNotes:
Betta macrostoma is widely regarded as one of the crown jewels of the wild Betta hobby and one of the most challenging freshwater fish to keep successfully. It is endemic to Brunei Darussalam (and a small adjoining area of Sarawak, Malaysia), where it inhabits shaded blackwater forest streams of exceptional purity — extremely soft, highly acidic, and tannin-stained. Replicating these conditions is non-negotiable: pH 4.0–6.5, gH 0–3, and kH near zero must be maintained using RO or rain water buffered with Indian almond leaves, peat, or commercial blackwater extract. Any deviation toward harder or more alkaline conditions degrades health rapidly.

A dedicated species-only tank of at least 80 L per pair is essential; a tight-fitting lid is mandatory as B. macrostoma is a capable jumper. The tank should be heavily shaded (floating plants, subdued lighting), richly furnished with leaf litter, and filtered with a gentle sponge filter — these fish are intolerant of strong current. Regular small water changes with matched RO water are critical; large changes risk parameter shock.

B. macrostoma is listed as Vulnerable on the IUCN Red List and is subject to collection and export restrictions from Brunei. Virtually all available specimens are wild-caught, with significant mortality during shipping and the stress of transport. Acclimation must be extremely slow and careful. Price per pair commonly ranges from USD 150 to 500+ in the Western hobby trade.

Females and males must be introduced carefully; males display brilliant orange-red and black coloration when breeding. Pairs can be kept together once established but should be monitored for female harassment. The species is best kept exclusively with conspecifics or in a species-only setup.
breedingNotes:
Betta macrostoma is a paternal mouthbrooder — one of the largest and most impressive in the genus. Spawning is preceded by extended courtship during which the male displays intensely colored flanks and spread fins. The female releases a small batch of relatively large eggs (10–35 per spawning) which the male immediately collects and incubates orally for approximately 14–21 days. The male should not be fed during incubation to avoid accidental egg expulsion. Fry are released as fully formed miniatures roughly 6–8 mm in length and are able to accept live micro-worms, baby brine shrimp nauplii, and infusoria from the outset.

Breeding requires stable, optimal blackwater conditions and a stress-free environment; any disturbance may cause the male to swallow the eggs. Separate the female after spawning if she shows signs of harassment. Raising fry is possible but demanding: soft, acid water must be maintained, and live foods are essential for the first weeks of life. Successful captive breeding is uncommon but has been achieved by specialist keepers, and captive-bred specimens — when available — are greatly preferred over wild-caught stock for welfare and conservation reasons.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Betta macrostoma
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (~11 cm), temperature 22–28°C, pH 4.0–6.5, hardness 0–3 dH, native range (Brunei, Sarawak), biotope (blackwater forest streams), diet (invertebrates), breeding (paternal mouthbrooder, 14–21 day incubation, 10–35 fry), IUCN Vulnerable status, and husbandry notes including species-only tank requirement and RO water necessity.

additional.name: FishBase — Betta macrostoma
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (to 11.0 cm TL), temperature range, native range (Borneo: Brunei and adjacent Sarawak), taxonomy (Osphronemidae, Anabantiformes), IUCN status (Vulnerable VU A2cd), and diet (invertivore). Confirmed Brunei endemic status and restricted distribution.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Betta macrostoma profile
additional.notes: Confirmed specialist-only care requirements: extreme blackwater parameters, paternal mouthbrooding behaviour, high price and limited availability of wild-caught specimens, species-only tank recommendation, and tight-fitting lid requirement. Also noted male breeding coloration (vivid orange and black) and female submission posture during courtship.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Wild Betta Species Guide
additional.notes: General wild Betta overview noting blackwater specialist requirements, mouthbrooding genera, and care level for advanced/specialist Bettas including macrostoma group. Used for context on wild Betta husbandry standards and diet (live/frozen invertebrates). No dedicated B. macrostoma page available.

additional.name: IUCN Red List — Betta macrostoma (VU A2cd)
additional.notes: Confirmed IUCN Vulnerable status (VU A2cd) due to habitat loss (deforestation, peat fires) and over-collection for the ornamental fish trade. Restricted endemic range in Brunei and adjacent Sarawak. Population declining. Supports hobbyistAdvisory specialist-only designation.

fish.breedingNotes: Paternal mouthbrooder; male incubates 10–35 large eggs for 14–21 days without feeding. Requires stable, pristine blackwater conditions (pH 4.0–6.5, gH 0–3) and a stress-free species-only tank. Fry are large and accept live micro-worms and BBS nauplii from release. Successful captive breeding is uncommon; captive-bred stock is rare and preferred over wild-caught.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Brunei endemic; extreme soft-acid blackwater requirements (pH 4.0–6.5, gH 0–3); paternal mouthbrooder with demanding breeding protocol; expensive wild-collected stock ($150–500+ per pair) with high mortality during shipping; IUCN Vulnerable — captive-bred stock strongly preferred but rarely available.
